speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We were in a barn. The barn had two sides-- on each side of the barn there was a haystack. In the middle, there was a concrete where you could put horses, but there were no horses at the time. And the reason I say that is because later on in my story, it's significant why I mention it. Our hole was near the outside wall of the barn, right near the slats of the barn. And it was very small. We were all sitting, squashed together. My father was always in a bending position. I, as a child, could sit normally, but he had to have his head always bent down because it wasn't that big. You know, it was just a very small area for the three of us. If one wanted to turn all three of us had to turn the same time. And as far as clothing and washing, we wore the same clothes for 18 months. We never washed our hands or face once. Pavlo, the only way he would get near us, he had a dog named [? Briskov ?] and he would whistle-- that dog saved our lives. Every time that he wanted to go near us he would whistle for the dog. And the dog would come and he would say something, whisper to the dog, and the dog would go running around, all around the farm, and then come back. And if he told him nobody was around, that's when he would come near us. So that dog was our life saver. If someone was nearby, that dog would be barking. He wouldn't go near us. So her was safer to go-- he could go near us.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had on a huge coat with pockets, and he would put the food in his pocket. And he couldn't give us much because he was very poor. A piece of bread. maybe a little bit of soup, a bottle of water. And he would try once a day or maybe every other day-- come into the barn like he would be getting hay out for the horses, you know? He had always-- not just to come in. He always came out doing something that it wouldn't be suspicious for anybody. And he would give us that stuff.

speaker: interviewee
text: And once a night he gave us a pot for elimination and that was the most-- that was the most dangerous part of the whole 18 months of hiding because once a night the same thing. He would call the dog, [? Briskov ?], and the dog would be running around all over, making sure there was no one around and-- and he would go from the outside of the barn, Pavlo, and take out a piece of the sled. And my father would crawl out with the pot and not-- not far from there there was a little river, and he would throw it into the river. And-- but it was a very dangerous thing to do because you never knew if anybody-- even though the dog-- you know, it's still a dog. And so when he could-- of course, when he couldn't it would go on for more than a day-- or, at night. It was usually at that that would happen. The piece of bread that we had, we had to share with mice. The mice were always around. They were our best buddies. They wanted the bread. You had to hold it like this, otherwise they would grab it out of your hand. And I was very fearful always of mice, and-- but you get used to it. Lice. Like, unbelievable. We were covered with lice. By 3 o'clock you have to know we had no light at all. The only light we had was between the slats of the wood. And-- and in order for my father and mother and myself to keep sane, my father would teach me the Bible by heart. I knew the whole Bible by heart. Every day, you know, he would teach me something else. And when he would forget a word I would remind him. So I became, like, very religious, always reciting something from the Bible. And also Pavlo-- my mother asked for wool, and she started knitting. I don't know how she was knitting with all the mice and the lice, but I don't know. She was knitting. I remember her knitting all kinds of sweaters for the girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father, [? he ?] wood [? boot. ?] And my father would carve shoes from wood. Just to do something. And then the children would go to church, and everybody wanted to know, where did-- who made you those beautiful shoes and who made you-- you know? He had to create all kinds of stories for that. But they were busy up to 3 o'clock in the afternoon. Then it was pitch black. And this is how we survived. It's-- it's-- like, if someone would come and tell me, I would never believe that anyone could live this way for 18 months, but we did. We lived for 18 months with very little food, no water except just a drink to wet our lips, you know? And the pot and the mice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, six weeks before liberation, would you believe that the German soldiers made their [? astent ?] in our barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: They made their [? astent ?]. Because we were near a little river. So the barn is where they made their [? astent ?]. They took over the farm. It was for six weeks. It was unbelievable. The German soldiers slept on top of our-- on top of the hay. And at that time Pavlo insisted that he put their horses in the barn. And the Germans didn't want to. They said, it's OK. And he said, no, no, no. It's much better for you to have the horses here. They're warmer, they're safer. He gave them all kinds of excuses because he wanted to have some kind of movement. If they would hear something, they should think it's the horses. He needed something for a distraction. So he was very busy with the horses. There were a whole bunch of German soldiers on top of us and we could hear them inhale a cigarette. And for the six weeks, my father had his hand on my mouth and nose constantly. God forbid you want to sneeze or you want to cough or anything, and we heard-- I-- I can still remember hearing them inhaling. Of course, they were laughing and talking, but smoking a cigarette we could hear. Of course, it-- it wasn't a very high hay stack. I mean, it was OK, but it wasn't huge. And they were on top and we were on the bottom. And it's like, when I think about it, it's, like, unbelievable that it could happen. That we could survive. I don't even know how he gave us food in those six weeks that they were there. He took such chances. And of course my father had to empty out the pot. I don't know how-- I don't rem-- I don't know. It was-- it was just a miracle. It was just a miracle. And of course when they retreated-- you know, the war was over, they retreated. And we still-- we stayed there with him for a few weeks at least. Because, first of all, he was afraid for his neighbors. Should they have known that they saved Jews, they would have killed him with his family. His own neighbors. Plus the fact there were many Jews killed afterwards. As soon as they were coming out from their hiding places into town, into [? Tuchen ?], they would be-- they would be slaughtered.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he kept us for a few more weeks to make sure until things stabilized a little bit. And that's when we crawled out from the hole. And we couldn't even walk. We were holding onto sticks for support. And-- I'm sorry. I'm sorry, we had to stop for noise outside. But continue with telling me when you got out of the hole.

speaker: interviewee
text: I also want to mention before how I felt as a child in-- during the time, I was so envious of a bird outside. I-- [AUDIO OUT]
